title: Leslie Vs. April

jokes.text: 2020... Uh... that's a stretch. Fine. 2024.
jokes.setup: Ben suggests they could be in the White House by 2020
jokes.punchline: Leslie's matter-of-fact assessment that even 2024 is ambitious
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Leslie's ambitious but realistic nature. Great economy and timing.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: We could be in the White House by 2020.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Even 2024 is ambitious.

jokes.text: I'll take the West Wing. You take the East Wing. You can be the first gentleman.
jokes.setup: Planning their future White House living arrangements
jokes.punchline: Casually assigning Ben the role of 'first gentleman'
jokes.explanation: Shows Leslie's assumption she'll be president and Ben's acceptance of it. Character-driven humor.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: When we're in the White House, you'll be the First Gentleman.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I think you mean First Lady.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: No, First Gentleman. You're a gentleman, and you'll be first.

jokes.text: Is it a waffle tower?
jokes.setup: Ben mentions getting her an engagement present
jokes.punchline: Leslie's specific and absurd guess reveals her obsessions
jokes.explanation: Pure Leslie moment - her brain goes to waffles immediately. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I got you an engagement present.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Is it a solid gold statue of me? Or a lifetime supply of waffles? Or a solid gold waffle of me?

jokes.text: You're--my name just came out of your mouth.
jokes.setup: Vice President Biden says her name
jokes.punchline: Leslie's awkward, star-struck observation of the obvious
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Leslie's starstruck inability to function normally. Great cringe comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Did you guys hear that? He said my name. Vice President Biden said my name!

jokes.text: Oh, Mr. vice president, I am deeply flattered, but there's no way that I could take over Madam Secretary Clinton's position. I mean-- Okay, I will.
jokes.setup: Leslie starts to humbly decline what she thinks is a job offer
jokes.punchline: Immediately changes her mind mid-sentence
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie moment - her ambition overrides her humility instantly. Excellent structure and timing.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh, well, that's very flattering, but I'm not sure I'm really qualified for... wait, did you say Deputy Director? I'm in.

jokes.text: Hey-- y-you're very welcome. You're very handsome. Well, you're very nice. Okay. Thank you.
jokes.setup: Leslie trying to respond appropriately to the Vice President's thanks
jokes.punchline: Escalating awkwardness as she can't stop talking
jokes.explanation: Great cringe escalation showing Leslie's inability to quit while ahead. Strong character work.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You're welcome, Mr. Vice President. It was an honor to serve alongside you.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: And I just want to say, your leadership has been truly inspiring. The way you've handled environmental policy, it's really—it's really something special.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: And your tie today, it's a very nice shade of blue. Really brings out your—it brings out the blue in your eyes, which are very blue.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Anyway, I'm just going to stop talking now. So... thank you.

jokes.text: You don't let anything happen to him, you understand me? He is precious cargo.
jokes.setup: Leslie addressing Ben's safety
jokes.punchline: Treating Ben like he needs protection from the Vice President
jokes.explanation: Leslie's protective instincts are both sweet and absurd in this context.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm treating Ben like he needs protection from the Vice President.

jokes.text: So just call me Bond, municipal bond.
jokes.setup: Ben explaining his return to accounting
jokes.punchline: Attempting to make accounting sound cool with a James Bond reference
jokes.explanation: Classic Ben trying to make accounting exciting. The pun is predictable but fits his character.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I'm back in accounting. It's like James Bond, except instead of fighting supervillains, I'm fighting... spreadsheets. And instead of a martini, I have a cup of coffee. And instead of a tuxedo, I have this shirt from Banana Republic.

jokes.text: Yeah, that joke killed at the accounting firm.
jokes.setup: Ben's municipal bond joke
jokes.punchline: His proud admission that accountants found it hilarious
jokes.explanation: Meta joke about his own bad joke, plus perfect character moment showing Ben's nerd pride.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I made a joke about municipal bonds. The accountants laughed so hard, one of them had to sit down. I'm not saying it was the funniest thing ever said, but it was definitely top five, easily.

jokes.text: Well, well, well. If it isn't Mr. Looks and Professor Books. He's Looks. I got it.
jokes.setup: Tom giving Ben and Andy nicknames
jokes.punchline: Andy's proud understanding of which one he is
jokes.explanation: Andy's simple pleasure at being called good-looking is endearing and character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Ben, you're 'The Debt Tornado.' Andy, you're 'The Silly Goose.'

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yes! I'm the Silly Goose!

jokes.text: Well, fun fact: Ben just got an amazing accounting job. Regular fact: I have to go to a meeting. Un-fun fact: My Uncle just had a stroke.
jokes.setup: Leslie categorizing facts by how fun they are
jokes.punchline: The devastating family news casually delivered as 'un-fun'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant structure and dark turn. Leslie's systematic approach to even tragedy is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Fun facts: the Great Wall of China is visible from space, honey badgers are immune to snake venom, and your father had a second family in Manitoba.

jokes.text: We specialize in making stacks on stacks on stacks on stacks.
jokes.setup: Tom describing his new business
jokes.punchline: Vague business-speak that means nothing
jokes.explanation: Classic Tom meaningless business jargon. Character-appropriate but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I'm leveraging my core competencies to create a synergistic paradigm shift in the value-added solutions space.

jokes.text: I discovered I'm roughly the same size as the average Pawnee 12-year-old.
jokes.setup: Tom explaining his clothing rental business model
jokes.punchline: Admitting his small stature in a matter-of-fact way
jokes.explanation: Great self-deprecating humor that's also a logical business insight. Very Tom.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I rent out my clothes to other guys. It's a business model that works because I'm a very small man.

jokes.text: Pawnee only has two dog parks-- this one and that one, which is also an active parking lot.
jokes.setup: April describing Pawnee's dog park situation
jokes.punchline: One of the 'dog parks' is actually just a parking lot
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan observation about municipal incompetence. Solid structure.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: One of the 'dog parks' is actually just a parking lot.

jokes.text: Can you say 'per capita' again? I want to take a picture of you saying 'per capita.'
jokes.setup: April using the phrase 'per capita'
jokes.punchline: Andy's weird fascination with photographing her saying it
jokes.explanation: Pure Andy - finding something inexplicably fascinating about mundane things. Strong character work.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Per capita.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, say that again.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Per capita.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh man, that's so hot. Can you say it one more time while I take a picture?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Please? Per capita is my new thing.

jokes.text: And... here we go.
jokes.setup: Leslie getting emotional about April's potential
jokes.punchline: Her resigned acknowledgment that she's about to cry
jokes.explanation: Leslie knowing herself well enough to predict her emotional response. Character-driven.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm going to cry. I'm going to cry right now.

jokes.text: He's an animal living on a human farm. And you can go and feed him from your own hand.
jokes.setup: April describing Orin's performance art
jokes.punchline: The bizarre concept delivered matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Perfectly weird concept delivered in April's deadpan style. Strong absurdist humor.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Orin has been working on a performance art piece where he stands in the town square wearing a bikini made of raw chicken, and he just screams about his childhood for six hours straight.

jokes.text: I don't want to whip out the legalese on you now, but I got dibs.
jokes.setup: Leslie threatening legal action over the lot
jokes.punchline: Her 'legalese' is just calling dibs like a child
jokes.explanation: Perfect juxtaposition between formal legal language and playground rules. Very Leslie.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm prepared to take this all the way to court. I have documented everything in triplicate, filed the appropriate forms, and I'm ready to pursue this with the full force of municipal code section 47-B, paragraph 3, subsection...

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: ...which clearly states: 'Dibs.' I called dibs on this lot first, and according to the ancient laws of childhood, that makes it mine. Finders keepers, losers weepers.

jokes.text: A game is the foot.
jokes.setup: Andy trying to say 'the game is afoot'
jokes.punchline: Completely mangling the phrase
jokes.explanation: Classic Andy malapropism. Simple but perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: The game is a foot.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, no. The game is... afoot? No, that's not right either. The game is... a-foot? Like, the game has a foot?

jokes.text: I took my talents to South Beach.
jokes.setup: Donna explaining her Miami trip
jokes.punchline: Using LeBron James' famous phrase for a vacation
jokes.explanation: Basic pop culture reference without much twist. Fits Donna but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: I went to Miami for vacation. Best decision I ever made. I'm not going back to Pawnee. I'm taking my talents to South Beach.

jokes.text: Yes, I took your government computer from Indiana to Miami to sell it on the black market for 150 bucks.
jokes.setup: Andy's accusation about computer theft
jokes.punchline: Donna sarcastically admitting to an absurdly unprofitable crime
jokes.explanation: Good sarcastic response highlighting the absurdity of Andy's theory. Solid delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Yeah, I stole your computer. I'm running a very profitable business stealing government computers and selling them for seven dollars each on Craigslist.

jokes.text: Your mission statement made sense, and you didn't use one R. Kelly lyric.
jokes.setup: Ben praising Tom's business plan
jokes.punchline: Referencing Tom's past tendency to quote R. Kelly inappropriately
jokes.explanation: Nice callback that shows character growth while maintaining Tom's personality.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Tom, this business plan is actually really impressive. You've thought through all the details, the financials, the marketing strategy.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Thanks, Ben. I'm really proud of it.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Just one thing though—please tell me there are no R. Kelly lyrics in here.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Why would there be R. Kelly lyrics in a business plan?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Because the last time you got excited about something, you wouldn't stop quoting 'I believe I can fly' at the city council meeting.

jokes.text: It seems like the kind of place a ska band would go to shoot heroin.
jokes.setup: Ben describing the terrible lot condition
jokes.punchline: Oddly specific and random cultural reference
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly specific and weird observation. The ska band detail makes it memorable and quotable.

jokes.text: I mean, look, there's already a dog here. Oh, my God. I had that exact doll when I was a kid.
jokes.setup: April pointing out a 'dog' on the lot
jokes.punchline: Ann recognizing it as her childhood doll, implying it's creepy/dirty
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag setup with unexpected personal connection adding to the creepiness.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: What is that thing?

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Oh my God. That's my childhood doll.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Why would you leave that here?

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: I didn't! I threw it away years ago.

jokes.text: Okay. Saw off your pinky toe. No. Shave your head. No. Have sex with Jerry. No.
jokes.setup: April demanding increasingly extreme things from Leslie
jokes.punchline: The escalating absurdity, especially the Jerry suggestion
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation structure with April's dark humor. Jerry as the ultimate torture is character-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Leslie, I need you to get me a helicopter.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: A helicopter? April, that's not really—

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: And I need you to teach it to speak French.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, now you're just—

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Also, I want you to go back in time and prevent the invention of Mondays.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's literally impossible.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: And finally, I need you to make Jerry the mayor of a small European country.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: April, why would you—

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Actually, that's the only reasonable one.

jokes.text: I have created a monster. And now I need to destroy her.
jokes.setup: Leslie realizing April has become too good at her job
jokes.punchline: Treating it like a Frankenstein situation
jokes.explanation: Great dramatic language for a mundane workplace situation. Very Leslie melodrama.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I've created a monster. April has become too efficient, too capable. She's like Frankenstein's monster, except instead of terrorizing villagers, she's just... perfectly organizing my files and anticipating my needs before I even know I have them.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: That's the job description.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I know, but I didn't think you'd actually be GOOD at it. Now I'm afraid if I say the wrong thing, you'll turn on me. I've lost control of my creation.

jokes.text: He's smart, and he's beautiful, and I think of him in many ways as a daughter, but that would be crazy 'cause he's a man and his name's Ricky.
jokes.setup: Leslie describing 'Ricky' (April) to Ron
jokes.punchline: The confusing gender logic while trying to maintain the disguise
jokes.explanation: Leslie's convoluted logic while trying to maintain her fiction. Character-appropriate rambling.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: So Ricky is... well, he's very masculine. Deep voice, facial hair, very strong.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Sounds like a man.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: He is! Except when he needs to be a woman for the mission.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: That doesn't make any sense.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I know, but we're committed to the bit.

jokes.text: I once had you put together a brochure about different kinds of Indiana topsoil.
jokes.setup: Ron explaining how he used to manage Leslie
jokes.punchline: The absurdly specific busy work he gave her
jokes.explanation: Perfect example of Leslie being distracted by work she'd find genuinely interesting.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I used to give Leslie these incredibly specific tasks just to keep her busy. I'd say, 'Leslie, I need you to organize all the binders in this office by the number of sheets inside them, then cross-reference that with the color of the binder and create a spreadsheet.' She'd spend eight hours on it, and at the end of the day, I'd just throw it away.

jokes.text: That soil brochure was not busy work. I mean, people still refer to Mulch Ado About Nothing.
jokes.setup: Leslie defending her soil brochure
jokes.punchline: The Shakespeare pun title she's proud of
jokes.explanation: Brilliant pun that Leslie would absolutely be proud of. Perfect character moment with excellent wordplay.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I called it 'Much Ado About Mulching.' Get it? Because Shakespeare? And mulch?

jokes.text: Those were distraction waffles? I thought they were friendship waffles.
jokes.setup: Ron revealing his waffle strategy
jokes.punchline: Leslie's hurt realization about the true nature of their waffle dates
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback with emotional weight. Leslie's categorization of waffles by purpose is very her.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I've been going to JJ's Diner every morning for 20 years. Waffles are a stable, predictable food. Consistent. Reliable.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, those waffle mornings have been some of my favorite memories with you.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I know. That's why I keep doing them. But I go there for the waffles, not the company.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wait... you're saying our special waffle tradition was just... about the waffles?

jokes.text: Breakfast food can serve many purposes.
jokes.setup: Leslie's emotional reaction to waffle revelation
jokes.punchline: Ron's matter-of-fact wisdom about food utility
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron response - practical wisdom delivered deadpan. Great economy and character voice.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Leslie, I've been eating waffles since before you were born. They're just a delivery system for syrup and butter. I don't need them to change my life.

jokes.text: I like the statement it's making that, you know, we're all in cages in a way. Yeah, it's actually about death and the city.
jokes.setup: Ann trying to intellectually engage with Orin's art
jokes.punchline: Orin correcting her with an even more pretentious interpretation
jokes.explanation: Good satire of pretentious art interpretation. Ann trying to fit in vs Orin's actual weirdness.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: So this piece is about the commodification of the human form in late capitalism?

dialogue.speaker: Orin
dialogue.line: No, it's about the commodification of the human form in late capitalism AND the male gaze.

jokes.text: Oh, my escargot delivery service, Snail Mail.
jokes.setup: Reference to Tom's previous business idea
jokes.punchline: The pun-based business name
jokes.explanation: Classic Tom pun-based business idea. Simple but character-appropriate.

jokes.text: People died. Actually, speaking of, we're starting up a non-profit foundation to restore our name.
jokes.setup: Discussing the molasses disaster casualties
jokes.punchline: Seamlessly transitioning from death to corporate PR
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark corporate mindset - treating deaths as a natural segue to PR strategy. Excellent structure.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: We need to address the molasses disaster victims with compassion and dignity.

dialogue.speaker: Jessica
dialogue.line: Absolutely. We should launch a comprehensive memorial initiative.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: I'm thinking a candlelit vigil, a scholarship fund in their names...

dialogue.speaker: Jessica
dialogue.line: Or we could rebrand it as a 'Molasses Incident Awareness Month' with corporate sponsorships and a hashtag campaign.

jokes.text: Just to clarify, it's a no to me on all fronts, and you're offering Ben a job? Yeah. Cool. Cool.
jokes.setup: Tom being rejected while Ben gets offered a job
jokes.punchline: Tom's forced casual acceptance of the slight
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment showing Tom's ego taking a hit while trying to maintain composure.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Yeah, no, that's cool. Ben's great. Ben's really great. I'm happy for Ben.

jokes.text: It's not. My real name is Gary. Well, Gary--if that even is your real name--
jokes.setup: Jerry revealing his real name isn't Jerry
jokes.punchline: Andy immediately doubting the new name too
jokes.explanation: Good running gag with Andy's detective paranoia extending to the correction itself.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: My real name isn't Jerry. It's Garry.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Garry? That's like a angry way to say Jerry.

jokes.text: I can never tell when people are lying to me. Hopefully that doesn't come up in my police work.
jokes.setup: Andy's investigation difficulties
jokes.punchline: His oblivious concern about a crucial police skill he lacks
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy moment - completely missing the obvious problem with his career choice.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I've been thinking about becoming a cop, but I realized I don't know how to do a stakeout. Like, I don't even know what you're supposed to do on a stakeout.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: You sit in a car and watch a location.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: For how long?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: As long as it takes.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: That's the problem. I can't just sit somewhere. I need to be doing something. I need to be moving around, talking to people, eating food.

jokes.text: What's your favorite part? The heavy-handedness.
jokes.setup: Leslie claiming to love Orin's art show
jokes.punchline: April sarcastically praising the least subtle aspect
jokes.explanation: Great meta joke about pretentious art while maintaining April's deadpan sarcasm. Excellent economy.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Orin's art show was absolutely incredible. The way he captured the essence of despair and emptiness was just breathtaking.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Yeah, and I really appreciated how he used the color black. Very subtle choice.

jokes.text: He gave me a really cool dental mirror to check out my molars.
jokes.setup: April explaining why she trusts Jamm
jokes.punchline: Being impressed by a basic dental tool
jokes.explanation: Shows April's inexperience with political manipulation. Character-appropriate naivety.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I trust Jeremy Jamm because he's the only person in this town who's more of a sociopath than I am.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Plus, he showed me his water flosser once and I was genuinely impressed.

jokes.text: Moo. Moo. Quiet, weirdo. Aren't you supposed to be a sheep? No. You are.
jokes.setup: April and Orin in animal costumes
jokes.punchline: Arguing about their animal assignments while in character
jokes.explanation: Absurd situation comedy. The commitment to arguing while in character adds to the humor.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I'm not wearing this stupid raccoon outfit.

dialogue.speaker: Orin
dialogue.line: But April, you're perfect as a raccoon. They're trash pandas.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Exactly. Why would you assign me that?

dialogue.speaker: Orin
dialogue.line: I'm a llama. Do you know how humiliating that is?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: At least llamas are cool. Raccoons are literally garbage.

dialogue.speaker: Orin
dialogue.line: We could trade.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: No. You're staying a llama, and you're going to like it.

jokes.text: Not to mention in the last month alone three people have confused me for a shorter, Indian Ryan Gosling.
jokes.setup: Tom building himself up to the reporter
jokes.punchline: The oddly specific and backhanded comparison
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tom self-promotion that includes qualifiers that undermine the compliment. Very in character.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I'm like the Beyoncé of government. Not in terms of talent or influence, but in terms of how many times I've been unfaithful to my wife.

jokes.text: He sweats. He stutters. It's like The King's Speech, but the first part, before he's fixed.
jokes.setup: Tom criticizing Ben's TV performance
jokes.punchline: Comparing him to the worst part of an inspirational movie
jokes.explanation: Clever reference with a twist - focusing on the unflattering part of the story. Good structure.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Ben, you were like the worst part of an inspirational movie. You know, the part where they explain the problem for forty-five minutes.

jokes.text: That was my favorite part of the movie.
jokes.setup: Tom's criticism of The King's Speech's beginning
jokes.punchline: Cameraman genuinely preferring the struggling part
jokes.explanation: Unexpected perspective from documentary crew member. Good deadpan delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: The King's Speech is a terrible movie. It's basically two hours of a guy struggling to say words.

dialogue.speaker: Cameraman
dialogue.line: Yeah, but that's the best part.

jokes.text: Don't play stupid and handsome with me. You're as guilty as you are sexy.
jokes.setup: Andy interrogating Chris
jokes.punchline: Accusations that are also compliments about appearance
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Andy logic - trying to be tough while giving compliments. Perfect character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Chris, you're so incredibly handsome it's basically a crime.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Thank you?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: No, I'm serious. You're aggressively good-looking. It's unfair to the rest of us.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I... appreciate that, Andy.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Your teeth are so white and perfect, it's honestly insulting. You're making everyone else look bad just by existing.

jokes.text: Like a real crime? That's even better.
jokes.setup: Learning City Hall was actually robbed
jokes.punchline: Andy's excitement about real crime vs. practice
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy reaction - more excited about real stakes than concerned about actual crime.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, wait. So City Hall was actually robbed? Like for real?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: This is so cool! We've been practicing for this our whole lives and it finally happened!

jokes.text: Really? I just heard one hag booing.
jokes.setup: Jamm claiming considerable opposition
jokes.punchline: April's blunt assessment of the 'opposition'
jokes.explanation: Classic April directness cutting through political BS. Good economy and character voice.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: There is no opposition. Jeremy Jamm is just a idiot who thinks he's important.

jokes.text: You don't even have to be Asian to do math that simple.
jokes.setup: Jamm explaining the financial benefits of selling to Paunch Burger
jokes.punchline: Casual racist comment delivered matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Shows Jamm's character as casually offensive but relies on cheap stereotype humor. Low craft score for lazy writing.

jokes.text: You told me you wanted a dog park. Uh, psych.
jokes.setup: April confronting Jamm about his lie
jokes.punchline: Jamm's childish 'psych' response to serious deception
jokes.explanation: Shows Jamm's immaturity and lack of professionalism. Simple but character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: You lied to me about the entire thing.

dialogue.speaker: Jamm
dialogue.line: Psych!

jokes.text: You just got jammed.
jokes.setup: Jamm's successful deception
jokes.punchline: His signature catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Established running gag. Low originality but strong character integration and quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Jamm
dialogue.line: And that's why you never trust a Jamm.

jokes.text: I hate when he says that.
jokes.setup: Jamm's 'you just got jammed' line
jokes.punchline: Leslie's exasperated reaction to his catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Good reaction beat showing Leslie's frustration. Simple but effective character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Jeremy Jamm
dialogue.line: You just got jammed!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Oh my God, can you please stop saying that?

jokes.text: Yes, you do. You're a mouse eater. Mouse eater. Mouse eater.
jokes.setup: Leslie denying she eats mice
jokes.punchline: April's insistent repetition of the absurd accusation
jokes.explanation: April's commitment to absurd insults. The repetition adds to the childish argument dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: You eat mice. You eat mice and you always have.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I've seen you do it.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: You're a mouse eater, Leslie.

jokes.text: No one leaves the Octagon. I dated an ultimate fighter. It was like a thing he said.
jokes.setup: Ann trying to sound tough by referencing the Octagon
jokes.punchline: Revealing it's just something her ex used to say
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ann moment - trying to be tough but revealing personal dating history instead.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: You better watch out, because I'm going to take you to the Octagon.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: That's where I'm going to destroy you.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: My ex-boyfriend used to say that all the time.

jokes.text: Boom, take 'em all out.
jokes.setup: Andy suggesting explosive computer traps
jokes.punchline: Casually suggesting murdering thieves
jokes.explanation: Shows Andy's action movie mindset applied to real police work. Character-appropriate overreach.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You know what? If someone steals my laptop, I'm gonna booby-trap my next one. You know, like Home Alone style.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: That's... that's illegal, Andy.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: What? It's my laptop!

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: You can't set traps that could seriously injure or kill someone.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Not even a little bit?

jokes.text: It's not superhero time.
jokes.setup: Andy's elaborate crime-fighting fantasies
jokes.punchline: Officer's blunt reality check
jokes.explanation: Good reality check for Andy's fantasies. Simple but effective deflation.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm gonna be the greatest police officer this town has ever seen. I'll solve crimes with my bare hands, rappel down buildings, maybe even fight a robot.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Andy, you haven't even passed the police academy yet.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Details, Jerry. Details.

jokes.text: Maybe we should find the person who stole his positive attitude.
jokes.setup: Andy getting discouraged about police work
jokes.punchline: Officer treating his mood change like another theft case
jokes.explanation: Clever observation about Andy's deflation framed as police work. Good economy and structure.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I don't know if police work is for me. Maybe I'm not cut out for this.

dialogue.speaker: Police Officer
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa. What happened to the Andy Dwyer who came in here full of energy and optimism?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: He's still here, he's just... discouraged.

dialogue.speaker: Police Officer
dialogue.line: Well, we're going to treat this like any other case. I'm going to need you to describe what was taken.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: What do you mean?

dialogue.speaker: Police Officer
dialogue.line: Your confidence. Someone stole your confidence. And we're going to find them.

jokes.text: If it was remotely interesting, there would be a show on A&E about it.
jokes.setup: Ben defending accounting from being called boring
jokes.punchline: Using TV programming as the measure of interest
jokes.explanation: Clever observation about reality TV culture while defending his profession. Good structure and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Accounting is not boring. If accounting were a TV show, it would be on HBO, not some basic cable network. It would have complex characters, intricate plotlines, and it would make you think.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ben, accounting would be on C-SPAN at 3 AM, and it would still get better ratings than C-SPAN's usual programming.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: That's fair.

jokes.text: They have a show about storage unit auctions.
jokes.setup: Ben's claim about A&E not covering boring topics
jokes.punchline: Pointing out an obviously mundane show that exists
jokes.explanation: Good comeback that undermines Ben's point. Simple but effective counter-argument.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: A&E doesn't cover boring topics.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: They have a show called 'Storage Wars.'

jokes.text: Yes. We'll have triple-sex with him.
jokes.setup: Ann suggesting they play dirty with Jamm
jokes.punchline: April's misunderstanding of what 'playing dirty' means
jokes.explanation: Classic April misunderstanding with sexual frankness. The 'triple' modifier makes it even more absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: We need to play dirty with Jamm.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Okay, I'll go roll around in the mud.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: No, that's not what I meant.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Too late, I'm already covered in mud.

jokes.text: When I get bummed out, I take my shirt off because the bad feelings make me feel sweaty.
jokes.setup: Andy starting to remove his shirt
jokes.punchline: His bizarre logic connecting emotions to clothing
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy logic - creating his own emotional processing system. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. If I'm sad, I need to take off my shirt because the sadness needs to escape through my torso.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: And if I'm happy, I gotta keep it on so the happiness doesn't fly away.

jokes.text: And I get a gun, and I can point it in people's faces. Incorrect.
jokes.setup: Andy's excitement about security guard job benefits
jokes.punchline: Chris's immediate factual correction about gun privileges
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between Andy's enthusiasm and Chris's precise correction. Strong timing.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Dude, I'm gonna get a gun! I'm gonna be packing heat!

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Actually, security guards in Indiana aren't authorized to carry firearms without additional licensing and training that this position doesn't provide.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: So... no gun?

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: No gun.

jokes.text: He is dry-humping my garden gnome. Stupid beast.
jokes.setup: Dogs playing in Jamm's yard
jokes.punchline: Jamm's horrified description of inappropriate dog behavior
jokes.explanation: Visual gag that works for the situation. Jamm's outrage adds to the comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Jeremy Jamm
dialogue.line: Oh my God! Oh my God! They're doing it right there on my lawn! In broad daylight! This is... this is a disaster! My neighbors are going to think I'm running some kind of... of... animal brothel!

jokes.text: You just got knoped. And ludgated. And perkinsed.
jokes.setup: Successfully outmaneuvering Jamm
jokes.punchline: Creating their own versions of Jamm's catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Good callback and reversal of Jamm's catchphrase. Team victory moment with character names as verbs.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, so we all agree that Jeremy Jamm is the worst, right?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Obviously.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: He's terrible.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, I've been thinking about his stupid catchphrase, 'Treat Yo' Self.' What if we created our own versions?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Like what?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: 'Treat Yo' Staff.' Because we should treat our staff well.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: 'Treat Yo' Health.' Get a checkup.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: 'Treat Yo' Pets.' Because animals matter.

jokes.text: Nice try. It didn't work. You just got jammed.
jokes.setup: The team thinking they won
jokes.punchline: Jamm still claiming victory with his catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Running gag continuation. Less impactful due to repetition but maintains character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Jamm
dialogue.line: I won! Treat yo' self!

jokes.text: Wait, wait, wait, do you never just take a second to enjoy things? I just said, 'let's get to work.' How else do people enjoy things?
jokes.setup: April questioning Leslie's immediate return to work
jokes.punchline: Leslie's genuine confusion about other forms of enjoyment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie moment - work IS her enjoyment. Excellent character insight delivered as comedy.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: You just had a baby. Shouldn't you be home enjoying your family?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Enjoy? April, I don't understand. There are zoning permits that need reviewing.

jokes.text: Well, formulas are my formula for moolah.
jokes.setup: Ben quitting accounting again
jokes.punchline: His attempt at a clever business saying
jokes.explanation: Ben trying to sound business-savvy with wordplay. Character-appropriate but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I'm quitting accounting. I'm going to start my own business venture.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: You've got to spend money to make money. It's just business 101.

jokes.text: Ted, get in here! Ben's quitting again, but you gotta hear what he said.
jokes.setup: Ben's formula/moolah line
jokes.punchline: Barney being genuinely impressed by Ben's weak wordplay
jokes.explanation: Good reaction showing how low the bar is for humor at accounting firms. Meta joke about Ben's bad joke.

jokes.text: I mean, other than the stability and the health plan and the above-average pay. Oh, God, this better work out.
jokes.setup: Ben explaining why he's leaving accounting
jokes.punchline: Immediately listing all the benefits he's giving up and panicking
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ben anxiety moment. The immediate second-guessing after bold proclamation is very character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I'm leaving accounting to pursue something more fulfilling.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. Do you know what I'm giving up? Dental. Full dental coverage. And vision. What if I go blind? I need to see things!

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: And the 401k match — they match up to 6%! Six percent! That's free money! Who leaves free money?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Oh God, I just realized I lose my parking spot. My spot. The one by the entrance.

tmdb_overview: Leslie gets upset when April tries to get Lot 48 made into a dog park. Meanwhile, Tom enlists Ben's help as he tries to launch his new business venture, and Andy hones his crime scene investigation skills.
insight: Leslie-April conflict fuels 67 jokes in 24 minutes, anchoring Season 5's tonal shift.
